message.content:
The fog on Blackwood Point didn’t just obscure vision; it swallowed sound. For Elias Thorne, the keeper of the lighthouse, the silence was a physical weight, heavier than the brass lenses he polished every evening. He had been alone here for twelve years, since the coastal authority automated the beacon and left him as a "legacy observer," a title that felt less like an honor and more like a polite eviction notice.

His days were a monastic routine: check the gears, log the weather, watch the gray expanse of the Atlantic churn against the cliffs. He spoke to no one but the gulls, and they only answered with shrieks.

It was on a Tuesday, during a storm that rattled the iron shutters, that he found it.

Elias had gone down to the shingle beach to check the erosion markers. The tide was unusually low, exposing a tangle of kelp and debris. Half-buried in the wet sand, glinting dully under the storm clouds, was a glass bottle. It was old, thick-walled, with a wax-sealed cork.

He picked it up. It was heavy, solid. Back in the warmth of the lantern room, with the great beam sweeping out over the dark water, he pried the cork loose. Inside was a single sheet of parchment, preserved by the wax and the glass.

He unfolded it, expecting a plea for help, a pirate’s treasure map, or perhaps a lover’s lament. Instead, he found a list.

*To the Keeper of Blackwood Point,*

*If you are reading this, you are Elias Thorne. You are thirty-eight years old. You are afraid that your life has passed you by while you watched the sea. You are wrong.*

*On November 14th, at 3:00 AM, a fishing
